title: Fundamental Rights vs Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSPs)
rows: Nature
Negative obligations on the State (restrict arbitrary power).
Positive directives to the State (command action for public welfare).
Justiciability
Justiciable (enforceable by courts under Articles 32 and 226).
Non-justiciable (cannot be directly enforced in court under Article 37).
Objective
Establishes political democracy in the country.
Establishes social and economic democracy (Welfare State).
Legal Effect
Have legal sanctions; violating laws can be declared unconstitutional.
Have moral and political sanctions; require legislative enactments to take effect.
Scope
Promotes individual welfare; protects citizen rights against state high-handedness.
Promotes community welfare; balances public good against individual interests.
Origin
Borrowed from the Constitution of the USA (Bill of Rights).
Borrowed from the Irish Constitution of 1937.

title: Directive Principles vs Fundamental Duties
rows: Target Audience
Directives given to the State (Government and authorities).
Moral and civic responsibilities mandated for Citizens of India.
Constitutional Location
Part IV (Articles 36–51).
Part IVA (Article 51A).
Origin & Inception
In the original Constitution (1950); borrowed from Ireland.
Added by 42nd CAA 1976 (Swaran Singh Committee); borrowed from USSR.
Core Objective
Guide state policies toward socio-economic welfare.
Remind citizens of their duties while exercising fundamental rights.
Total Count
Spread over 16 articles (36 to 51).
11 duties enlisted under a single article (51A).
Enforcement
Implemented via executive schemes and parliamentary legislation.
Enforced indirectly via statutory laws (e.g., Wildlife Protection Act).

title: Socialistic Principles vs Gandhian Principles
rows: Core Ideology
Democratic Socialism: elimination of inequalities and equitable distribution.
Mahatma Gandhi's vision: rural empowerment, moral reform, and village autonomy.
Focus Area
Workers' rights, fair wages, public assistance, and legal equality.
Grassroots democracy, cottage industries, SC/ST welfare, and prohibition.
Primary Aim
Prevent concentration of wealth and protect working classes.
Reconstruct rural Indian society through decentralized self-reliance.
Key Examples
Article 39(d) Equal pay for equal work; Article 39A Free legal aid.
Article 40 Village Panchayats; Article 43 Cottage industries; Article 47 Prohibition.
Economic Model
State-driven welfare and equitable public resource management.
Decentralized village-level self-sufficiency and co-operatives.

title: Positive Rights vs Negative Rights
rows: Definition
Require the State to act in a specific way to provide benefits or services.
Prohibit the State from performing certain actions that infringe on liberty.
State Obligation
Creates a positive duty for the State to create welfare conditions.
Imposes limits on legislative and executive power.
Implementation
Requires resources, policy interventions, and separate legislation.
Requires state restraint and non-interference.
Constitutional Examples
Article 39A (Free Legal Aid), Article 41 (Right to Work).
Article 14 (Equality before law), Article 21 (Protection of life/liberty).

title: Justiciable vs Non-Justiciable Provisions
rows: Court Enforcement
Directly enforceable by Supreme Court (Art 32) and High Courts (Art 226).
Courts cannot issue writs or orders to enforce them directly.
Legal Validity of Contraventions
Laws violating these provisions are void and unconstitutional.
Laws cannot be struck down solely for failing to fulfill these provisions.
Immediate Availability
Enjoyed automatically by citizens upon constitution's adoption.
Dependent on financial capability, state willingness, and enabling legislation.
Constitutional Domain
Fundamental Rights (Articles 12–35).
DPSPs (Articles 36–51) and Fundamental Duties (Article 51A).
